name: France
summary: Acute tech/AI shortage and full spouse work rights in the work phase, but a 47% Bangladeshi Schengen refusal rate, A2/B1/B2 French now layered across permits and citizenship, 12-month-only APS, and a sharp 2025 rise in anti-Muslim incidents weigh heavily on this couple.

links.title: France-Visas – International talents
Campus France – Tuition fees in France

job-market.summary: Acute SW/AI shortage: 4 in 5 firms struggle to hire, 15k+ cyber roles unfilled; Mistral/Datadog sponsor, English mainly in scale-ups.
job-market.reasoning: France has a severe software/AI talent shortage — about four in five firms report difficulty filling technical roles and 15,000+ cybersecurity posts sit unfilled in 2025, with AI now the single hardest skill to recruit globally. The French Tech Visa eases sponsorship at accredited firms (Mistral AI, Datadog, Doctolib, Dataiku), and AI engineers earn ~€70,000-120,000 (Mistral SWE €108k-142k), so both engineers can place; English-only roles cluster in scale-ups and multinationals while many employers still expect working French.
evidence: ~4 in 5 French firms report difficulty filling technical positions (2025)
15,000+ cybersecurity roles unfilled; AI named hardest skill to recruit globally (ManpowerGroup 2025)
AI engineers ~€70,000-120,000; Mistral AI SWE €108k (L1) to €142k (L3) in Paris
2026 French Tech Visa accredited employers include Mistral AI, Datadog, Criteo, Doctolib, Dataiku
Passeport Talent – Salarié Qualifié salary floor ~€38,616/yr, 4-year permit

links.title: France-Visas – International talents
Jobbatical – French Tech Visa employer certification

visa-access.summary: Processed in Dhaka (no New Delhi), €90 fee, ~€615/mo proof — but France refused 47.3% of Bangladeshi Schengen applicants in 2024.
visa-access.reasoning: Applications are handled in Dhaka via Campus France/Études en France and the French mission, so no New Delhi travel is needed, and fees are low (€90 long-stay; ~€615/month proof). But Bangladesh is one of the highest-refusal nationalities — France rejected 47.3% of Bangladeshi Schengen applicants in 2024 and Bangladesh's overall Schengen refusal hit 54.9% (up from 42.8% in 2023), signalling heavy document scrutiny even though long-stay student/Talent vetting differs from short-stay.
evidence: Campus France two-step (academic interview + visa section) decided in Dhaka; biometrics at the mission/VFS
Long-stay student visa fee €90; financial proof ~€615/month
France refused 47.3% of Bangladeshi Schengen visa applications in 2024 (highest among major issuers for BD)
Bangladesh overall Schengen refusal 54.9% in 2024, up from 42.8% in 2023 (vs 14.8% global average)

links.title: Bangladesh Campus France – Documents for visa
SchengenVisaInfo – highest 2024 rejection countries

citizenship.summary: 5 years + dual nationality allowed, but B2 French and a 40-question civic exam from Jan 2026, stricter prefectures, ~18-24mo processing.
citizenship.reasoning: Naturalisation needs five years' habitual residence and France permits dual nationality, matching the couple's no-renunciation preference. But Decree 2025-648 raised the bar to B2 French (oral and written) from 1 January 2026 and added a 40-question civic exam (32 correct to pass), while a May 2025 circular cut naturalisations 28% in 2024 — a real and rising barrier that lengthens the realistic path to 18-24 months plus B2 preparation.
evidence: Five years' habitual residence; dual nationality recognised (no renunciation required)
Decree 2025-648 (15 Jul 2025) requires B2 French from 1 Jan 2026, up from B1
New civic exam: 40 multiple-choice questions, 32 correct to pass (order of 10 Oct 2025)
Naturalisations granted fell ~28% in 2024 after stricter prefecture circulars
ANEF filing to Journal Officiel decree typically 18-24 months in 2026

links.title: Service-Public – Becoming French by naturalisation
Lexial – French immigration law changes 1 Jan 2026

post-study-work.summary: 12-month non-renewable APS/RÉCE for master's grads — the 24-month route is India-only; student-level work until a contract is signed.
post-study-work.reasoning: Master's and engineering graduates get the APS/RÉCE (Recherche d'emploi / Création d'entreprise) job-search permit, but for Bangladeshi nationals it is the standard 12 months, non-renewable — the 24-month version is a bilateral concession limited to Indian graduates, so the couple does not benefit. Work stays capped at student conditions (~964h/yr) until a contract is signed, then they switch into the Talent/employee permit — a workable but shorter, time-pressured bridge.
evidence: Standard APS/RÉCE for non-EU master's graduates: 12 months, non-renewable
24-month route applies only under bilateral agreements (e.g. India); Bangladesh gets 12 months
Work limited to student conditions (~964h/yr, ~20h/week) until a work contract is signed
Allows switch to Talent or salaried permit once hired

links.title: Campus France – Temporary resident permit (APS)
IAS – France post-study work (APS) graduate permit

spouse-family.summary: Work phase: Talent–Famille gives spouse immediate full work rights. Study phase: spouse on visitor visa cannot work — a real gap.
spouse-family.reasoning: Once on a Talent permit, the Talent–Famille card lets the accompanying spouse work freely with no salary threshold or separate sponsor — ideal for two engineers. But during the MSc study phase there is no clean route: a spouse arriving as a student's dependant typically gets a 'visiteur' visa that bars employment, and family reunification (which allows work) needs 18+ months' residence plus a minimum-income/accommodation test, so the second engineer is effectively idle through the study years.
evidence: Talent–Famille permit grants the spouse full work rights for the main permit's duration, no salary threshold
Study-phase accompanying spouse on a 'visiteur' visa is not authorised to work
Family reunification for students requires ~18 months' residence + minimum income (~€1,329/mo for 2-3 people) + accommodation
Talent–Famille processed in weeks vs months for classic reunification

links.title: Welcome to France – Talent: Qualified employees & family
Business France – Accompanying family members

msc-study.summary: 1,500+ English master's, Eiffel grants — but mandatory non-EU master's tuition jumps to €3,941/yr from Sept 2026; spouse can't work then.
msc-study.reasoning: France offers 1,500+ English-taught programmes plus the Eiffel scholarship (~€1,200/month from 2026) and student work rights, far cheaper than Anglophone destinations. But from September 2026 differentiated non-EU tuition becomes mandatory at ~€3,941/yr for master's (about 16x the old €254), with exemptions capped near 10%, and the spouse cannot work during the study phase — both materially dent the value for this dual-income couple.
evidence: 1,500+ English-taught degree programmes available
Mandatory non-EU master's tuition ~€3,941/yr from Sept 2026 (vs ~€254 previously); bachelor's ~€2,895
Exemptions capped near 10%; mostly scholarship/hardship cases
France Excellence Eiffel scholarship ~€1,200/month from Jan 2026; student work permitted

links.title: Campus France – Tuition fees in France
ICEF Monitor – France raises non-EU tuition from Sept 2026

pr-pathway.summary: 10-year carte de résident after ~5 years' legal residence; Talent gives a direct route, but B1 French + civic exam now required from 2026.
pr-pathway.reasoning: The 10-year carte de résident is generally available after five years' continuous legal residence, and Talent-status holders have a recognised direct path. From 1 January 2026 it requires B1 French and a passing civic exam (80% threshold) in addition to the Republican Integration Contract, adding friction; absences over six months can also reset continuity, so the route is predictable but no longer light-touch.
evidence: Carte de résident (10-year) after ~5 years' continuous legal residence
From 1 Jan 2026: B1 French + civic exam (80%) required for the resident card (Decree 2025-647)
First multi-year residence card now requires A2 French
Talent permit offers a direct path to permanent residence; application fee ~€225

Lexial – French immigration law changes 1 Jan 2026

income-cost.summary: Paris SW pay €50-78k gross, net ~73-78% after employee charges; but 1BR rent €1,800-2,000 and rent-to-income >36% erode savings.
income-cost.reasoning: Paris software engineers earn roughly €50,000-78,000 gross (seniors/AI higher), and employee-side deductions leave about 73-78% net — better than the previously assumed ~45%, which had conflated employer charges. However Paris rent runs €1,800-2,000 for a one-bedroom and the rent-to-income ratio exceeds 36%, so two engineering incomes deliver moderate, not strong, savings.
evidence: Paris software engineer ~€50,000-78,000 gross; France average ~€52,000-60,000
Net take-home ~73-78% of gross (e.g. €60k gross → ~€45-47k net)
Paris 1-bedroom city-centre rent ~€1,800-2,000/month; suburbs ~€1,300
Paris rent-to-income ratio >36% in 2025; single-person budget ~€2,300-2,500/month

links.title: Numbeo – Cost of living in Paris
Levels.fyi – Software Engineer salary, Greater Paris

healthcare.summary: Universal PUMA after ~3 months' residence; top-quality, ~84% costs prepaid, OOP ~9%; mutuelle usually needed for full cover.
healthcare.reasoning: France's Protection Universelle Maladie covers all legal residents after about three months and is consistently rated among the world's best for access, quality and wait times. About 84% of spending is mandatory prepayment and out-of-pocket sits near 9%, though cost-sharing on most services means residents typically buy a supplementary mutuelle, and only 60% report satisfaction with availability (OECD avg 64%).
evidence: PUMA covers all legal residents; access after ~3 months' residence
~84% of health spending mandatory-prepaid; out-of-pocket ~9% (OECD Health at a Glance 2025)
Unmet medical need very low (waiting lists ~1.0%, cost ~1.6% in 2021)
Most residents take a supplementary mutuelle (~€10-80/month) to cover cost-sharing

links.title: OECD – Health at a Glance 2025: France
WHO Euro Health Observatory – France health system summary

culture-language.summary: English works in tech and study, but A2/B1/B2 French now layered across permits and citizenship; French needed for daily admin.
culture-language.reasoning: English is usable in tech workplaces and English-taught study, but the 2026 reforms make French unavoidable across the whole pathway — A2 for the first card, B1 for the resident card, B2 plus a civic exam for citizenship — and daily prefecture/admin life runs in French. The escalating language ladder is the dominant cultural barrier for this English-working couple.
evidence: From 1 Jan 2026: A2 (first multi-year card), B1 (resident card), B2 (naturalisation)
Civic exam now required for both the resident card and naturalisation
French essential for daily life and prefecture/administrative dealings
English common in tech and across 1,500+ English-taught programmes eases entry only

links.title: Global Law Experts – France 2026 language requirements
Service-Public – Becoming French by naturalisation

safety-law.summary: WJP Rule of Law #22/143 (2025); strong institutions; urban petty theft and protests the main day-to-day concerns.
safety-law.reasoning: France ranks 22nd of 143 on the 2025 WJP Rule of Law Index with solid order/security and fundamental-rights scores. Violent crime is relatively low; pickpocketing on the Paris metro and around major stations plus periodic protests are the practical risks, while rising anti-Muslim incidents are a fairness concern weighed mainly under muslim-diaspora.
evidence: WJP Rule of Law Index 2025: France ranks 22 of 143
Paris crime concentrated in petty theft/pickpocketing; violent crime relatively low
Caution advised in parts of northern 18th/19th/20th arrondissements after dark

links.title: WJP Rule of Law Index 2025 – France (PDF)
WJP Rule of Law Index – France insights

politics.summary: Hung Assembly and government collapses since 2024, Rassemblement National rise, Jan 2024 immigration law, cascading 2025-26 tightening.
politics.reasoning: The 2024 snap elections produced a hung Assembly and repeated government collapses, while the far-right RN's influence shaped the January 2024 immigration law (much of it later censured by the Constitutional Council) and a steady tightening of residence/citizenship rules. With anti-immigrant sentiment rising and policy shifting against non-EU applicants, the environment is volatile and unfavourable for this profile.
evidence: January 2024 immigration law adopted with RN claiming a 'national preference' win; 35 of 86 articles censured
Hung National Assembly and recurring government collapses since 2024
Cascading tightening: A2/B1 permit language, B2 + civic exam for citizenship (2026), stricter circulars (2025)
Interior ministry pushed prefectures to cut naturalisations (~28% drop in 2024)

links.title: France24 – How France's far right changed the immigration debate
Library of Congress – France new immigration law 2024

tax.summary: Progressive IR 0-45% plus CSG/CRDS ~9.7%; engineer take-home ~73-78% of gross — heavy but not the extreme the old score implied.
tax.reasoning: France's 2026 income tax runs 0% to €11,600, 11%, 30%, 41% and 45% above €181,917, plus CSG/CRDS of ~9.7% on gross and a high-income surcharge. For a typical Paris engineer the effective burden is moderate-to-high — net pay lands around 73-78% of gross, not the ~45% previously stated (which mistakenly included employer charges) — still among the heavier OECD regimes.
evidence: CSG/CRDS ~9.7% of gross; employee social contributions ~22-25% total
Engineer net take-home ~73-78% of gross (e.g. €60k → ~€45-47k)
2026 Finance Act (signed 19 Feb 2026) raised thresholds ~0.9% for inflation

links.title: Service-Public – Income tax brackets
PwC – France individual taxes on personal income

muslim-diaspora.summary: Largest Muslim community in Europe (~9%), halal/mosques widespread — but anti-Muslim incidents +75% in early 2025, strict laïcité.
muslim-diaspora.reasoning: France has Europe's largest Muslim population (~9%) with abundant halal food and mosques, easing daily religious life. But laïcité bans religious symbols in state schools (abayas since 2023) and across several sports, and hostility is climbing sharply — anti-Muslim incidents rose 75% and assaults 209% in the first five months of 2025 — a serious and worsening concern for a visibly Muslim couple, pulling the score down.
evidence: ~9% of population Muslim — largest Muslim community in Europe; halal and mosques widespread
Anti-Muslim incidents +75% (83→145) and assaults +209% (32→99) in Jan-May 2025 vs 2024
1 in 3 Muslims report religious discrimination (French rights ombudsman)
Hijab/abaya banned in state schools; headscarf banned across football, basketball, volleyball; further bans debated 2025

links.title: Daily Sabah – 1 in 3 Muslims face rising discrimination in France
Amnesty International – France hijab ban concerns (2025)
